perturbation.original_text: Any late payments under this Agreement will be assessed a service fee of one and one-half percent (1.5%) per month, to the extent allowed by law.
perturbation.changed_text: Any late payments under this Agreement may be assessed a service fee, possibly around one and one-half percent (1.5%) per month, depending on the circumstances.
perturbation.explanation: The original text states a clear obligation regarding late payment fees. The modified text introduces ambiguity by replacing 'will be assessed' with 'may be assessed,' making the fee discretionary. The phrase 'possibly around' introduces further uncertainty about the fee amount, and 'depending on the circumstances' provides no concrete guidance, creating a vague and unenforceable obligation.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 1671 addresses the validity of liquidated damages clauses. The modified text's ambiguity surrounding late fees contradicts the requirement for a clear and reasonable estimate of damages in a contractual agreement, potentially rendering the late fee provision unenforceable under California law. A valid liquidated damages clause needs to be clear and not a penalty.

OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 4.5. LIQUIDATED DAMAGES [1671 - 1681] ( Heading of Title 4.5 added by Stats. 1977, Ch. 198. ) CHAPTER 1. General Provisions [1671- 1671.] ( Heading of Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1977, Ch. 198. ) 1671. (a) This section does not apply in any case where another statute expressly applicable to the contract prescribes the rules or standard for determining the validity of a provision in the contract liquidating the damages for the breach of the contract. (b) Except as provided in subdivision (c), a provision in a contract liquidating the damages for the breach of the contract is valid unless the party seeking to invalidate the provision establishes that the provision was unreasonable under the circumstances existing at the time the contract was made. (c) The validity of a liquidated damages provision shall be determined under subdivision (d) and not under subdivision (b) where the liquidated damages are sought to be recovered from either: (1) A party to a contract for the retail purchase, or rental, by such party of personal property or services, primarily for the party’s personal, family, or househ
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity regarding late payment fees, which contradicts the need for a clear liquidated damages clause under California law. The original text states a clear obligation using 'will be assessed,' whereas the changed text uses 'may be assessed,' making the fee discretionary. This undermines the clarity required for liquidated damages, as highlighted by California Civil Code § 1671, which implies that liquidated damages should be reasonable and not a penalty.

perturbation.original_text: Each party agrees to defend, indemnify and hold harmless the other and its shareholders, directors, officers, agents and employees for any and all losses, costs, liabilities or expenses (including without limitation reasonable attorneys' and expert witnesses' fees) incurred or arising from: (a) any breach of the foregoing representations or warranties; (b) any claim arising from the sale or license of either party's goods or services; or (c) any other act, omission or representation by either party.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party should endeavor to defend, indemnify and hold harmless the other and its shareholders, directors, officers, agents and employees for losses, costs, liabilities or expenses incurred or arising from: (a) breaches of the representations or warranties to a reasonable extent; (b) claims loosely associated with the sale or license of either party's goods or services; or (c) potentially relevant acts, omissions or representations by either party.
perturbation.explanation: The original text establishes a clear obligation for indemnification. The modified text weakens this by replacing 'agrees to defend, indemnify and hold harmless' with 'should endeavor to defend, indemnify and hold harmless,' making it a suggestion rather than a requirement. 'To a reasonable extent,' 'loosely associated with,' and 'potentially relevant' introduce ambiguity, making the scope of indemnification unclear and difficult to enforce.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 2778 outlines the rules for interpreting indemnity agreements. The modified text's ambiguity conflicts with the need for clear and definite terms in indemnity agreements, potentially leading to disputes and rendering the provision unenforceable under California law. Specifically, using terms like 'reasonable extent,' 'loosely associated,' and 'potentially relevant' make it hard to determine the scope of the indemnity, undermining the clarity required by law.

OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 4. OBLIGATIONS ARISING FROM PARTICULAR TRANSACTIONS [1738 - 3273.69] ( Part 4 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 12. INDEMNITY [2772 - 2784.5] ( Title 12 enacted 1872. ) 2778. In the interpretation of a contract of indemnity, the following rules are to be applied, unless a contrary intention appears: 1. Upon an indemnity against liability, expressly, or in other equivalent terms, the person indemnified is entitled to recover upon becoming liable; 2. Upon an indemnity against claims, or demands, or damages, or costs, expressly, or in other equivalent terms, the person indemnified is not entitled to recover without payment thereof; 3. An indemnity against claims, or demands, or liability, expressly, or in other equivalent terms, embraces the costs of defense against such claims, demands, or liability incurred in good faith, and in the exercise of a reasonable discretion; 4. The person indemnifying is bound, on request of the person indemnified, to defend actions or proceedings brought against the latter in respect to the matters embraced by the indemnity, but the person indemnified has the right to conduct such defenses, if he chooses to do so; 5. If, after reques
perturbation.justification: The modified text creates ambiguity around the indemnification obligation. The original text's 'agrees to defend, indemnify and hold harmless' is replaced with 'should endeavor to defend, indemnify and hold harmless', which weakens the obligation. Additionally, terms like 'to a reasonable extent,' 'loosely associated with,' and 'potentially relevant' introduce further ambiguity, making the scope of indemnification unclear. This directly undermines the clarity required for indemnity agreements, potentially rendering the provision unenforceable under California law, as highlighted by the Law Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 1, citing California Civil Code § 2778, emphasizes the importance of clarity in interpreting indemnity contracts; the ambiguities introduced in the modified text conflict with this requirement.

perturbation.original_text: Tickets agrees to accept MP3.com's measurement of Impressions (the "Count") according to MP3.com's logs and other tracking devices and/or software MP3.com may use, provided however, that Tickets shall have the right to audit MP3.com's records in this regard. If Tickets reasonably disputes the Count pursuant to this Agreement, then Tickets shall have the right to select the independent auditor of its choice to conduct an audit of MP3.com's records (the "Audit").
perturbation.changed_text: Tickets may consider accepting MP3.com's measurement of Impressions (the "Count") based on MP3.com's logs and other tracking methods MP3.com utilizes, but Tickets might have the option to audit MP3.com's records if deemed necessary. Should Tickets have some concerns regarding the Count pursuant to this Agreement, Tickets could potentially select an auditor to conduct an audit of MP3.com's records (the "Audit").
perturbation.explanation: The original text establishes Tickets' agreement to accept MP3.com's measurements, subject to audit rights. The modified text introduces significant ambiguity by replacing 'agrees to accept' with 'may consider accepting,' making acceptance discretionary. 'Might have the option' weakens the audit right, and 'some concerns' replaces 'reasonably disputes,' making the trigger for an audit vague. 'Could potentially select' further diminishes Tickets' power to select an auditor.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Commercial Code § 2311 addresses options and cooperation respecting performance. By making the acceptance of measurement and the right to audit discretionary, the modified text creates ambiguity around the performance standards, contradicting the requirement for reasonable cooperation and definiteness in commercial contracts. The vagueness introduced regarding the method of determining impressions and the conditions for audits disrupts the clear performance standards necessary for a good-faith commercial agreement.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, COM 2311 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Commercial Code - COM DIVISION 2. SALES [2101 - 2801] ( Division 2 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) CHAPTER 3. General Obligation and Construction of Contract [2301 - 2328] ( Chapter 3 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) 2311. (1) An agreement for sale which is otherwise sufficiently definite (subdivision (3) of Section 2204) to be a contract is not made invalid by the fact that it leaves particulars of performance to be specified by one of the parties. Any such specification must be made in good faith and within limits set by commercial reasonableness. (2) Unless otherwise agreed specifications relating to assortment of the goods are at the buyer’s option and except as otherwise provided in subdivisions (1)(c) and (3) of Section 2319 specifications or arrangements relating to shipment are at the seller’s option. (3) Where such specification would materially affect the other party’s performance but is not seasonably made or where one party’s co-operation is necessary to the agreed performance of the other but is not seasonably forthcoming, the other party in addition to all other remedies (a) Is excused for any resulting delay in his own performance; and (b) May also either proceed to perform in any reasonable manner or after the time for a material part of his own performance
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts California Commercial Code § 2311. The original text commits Tickets to MP3.com's measurement, while the changed text introduces ambiguity by stating Tickets 'may consider accepting,' making acceptance discretionary and weakening the obligation. Additionally, the right to audit is weakened from 'shall have the right to audit' to 'might have the option to audit,' and the trigger for an audit shifts from 'reasonably disputes' to 'some concerns,' creating further vagueness. This shift contradicts the requirement for reasonable cooperation and definiteness as indicated in snippet 1, which states an agreement is not invalid if it leaves particulars of performance to be specified by one of the parties, provided such specification is made in good faith and within limits set by commercial reasonableness. The modifications erode the definiteness and introduce subjectivity where objectivity existed before.
